21.title: Week 3 in one place — your speaking toolkit
21.intro: Everything from week 3 you need for today's free talk: biti, imati, ići, htjeti/moći + infinitive, ordering with the accusative, prices, time and adjective agreement. Read each line aloud — these are the building blocks of your 3-minute monologue.

items.hr: Ja sam Marko. Imam trideset godina.
items.en: I am Marko. I am thirty years old.
items.note: Age uses imati: imam + number + godina.

items.hr: Imam brata i sestru. Nemam auto.
items.en: I have a brother and a sister. I don't have a car.
items.note: imati is the only verb with a fused negative: nemam, nemaš, nema…

items.hr: Idem u grad. Ideš li i ti?
items.en: I'm going to town. Are you going too?
items.note: ići: idem, ideš, ide, idemo, idete, idu. u + accusative = motion to.

items.hr: Hoću učiti hrvatski. Mogu vježbati svaki dan.
items.en: I want to learn Croatian. I can practise every day.
items.note: htjeti (hoću) and moći (mogu) take a bare infinitive.

items.en: I'd like a coffee and a sandwich, please.
items.note: Accusative object: kava → kavu (f. -a → -u).

items.en: How much does it cost? — It costs five euros.
items.note: After pet the noun is in the genitive plural: pet eura.

items.hr: Koliko je sati? — Deset je sati.
items.en: What time is it? — It's ten o'clock.

items.hr: velik grad, velika kuća, veliko selo
items.en: a big city, a big house, a big village
items.note: Adjectives agree in gender: -ø (m), -a (f), -o (n).

items.hr: Svaki dan učim hrvatski i pijem kavu.
items.en: Every day I study Croatian and drink coffee.
items.note: A perfect monologue frame: Svaki dan + two present-tense verbs.

items.hr: Volim sport, ali ne volim televiziju.
items.en: I like sport, but I don't like television.
items.note: Negation: ne directly before the verb.

21.title: Week 3 mixed drill
21.intro: A cold re-test of week 3: conjugations, modal verbs + infinitive, the accusative and adjective agreement. Aim for 100% before the free talk.

questions.prompt: Oni ___ veliku kuću. (imati)
questions.explanation: 3rd person plural of imati is imaju: oni imaju.

questions.prompt: Ja ___ ići u kino. (htjeti — I want to go to the cinema)
questions.explanation: htjeti is irregular: hoću, hoćeš, hoće, hoćemo, hoćete, hoće. Note the ć.

questions.prompt: Mogu ___ sutra. (I can come tomorrow)
questions.explanation: After moći (and htjeti, morati…) the verb stays in the infinitive: mogu doći.

questions.prompt: Želim ___, molim. (kava — put it in the accusative)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a nouns take -u as the direct object: kava → kavu.

questions.prompt: What does 'Koliko košta?' mean?
questions.explanation: koliko = how much, koštati = to cost. Koliko je sati? asks the time.

questions.prompt: ___ kuća (a big house)
questions.explanation: kuća is feminine, so the adjective takes -a: velika kuća.

questions.prompt: Order a big coffee and a sandwich: (I want a big coffee and a sandwich)
questions.explanation: Verb + accusative object: veliku kavu (both adjective and noun take -u); sendvič is masculine inanimate, so it looks like the nominative.

questions.prompt: ___ vremena. (I don't have time — one word)
questions.explanation: imati fuses with ne: nemam vremena = I have no time.

questions.prompt: ja ___ (ići — present)
questions.explanation: ići has an irregular present stem: idem, ideš, ide, idemo, idete, idu.

21.title: Free talk: catching up with a friend
21.intro: Your weekly conversation. Act it out aloud with a partner (or both roles yourself), then improvise: swap in your own name, family and city. Only present tense — the past comes on day 28.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bok! Kako si? Dugo se ne vidimo.
lines.en: Hi! How are you? Long time no see.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Bok! Dobro sam, hvala. A ti?
lines.en: Hi! I'm fine, thanks. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: I ja sam dobro. Što radiš ovaj tjedan?
lines.en: I'm fine too. What are you doing this week?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Puno radim, ali svaki dan učim hrvatski.
lines.en: I'm working a lot, but I study Croatian every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično! Reci mi nešto o sebi.
lines.en: Excellent! Tell me something about yourself.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Ja sam Marko. Imam brata i sestru. Volim kavu i sport.
lines.en: I'm Marko. I have a brother and a sister. I like coffee and sport.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kakav je tvoj grad?
lines.en: What is your city like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Moj grad je velik i lijep. Imamo dobar restoran i malu školu.
lines.en: My city is big and beautiful. We have a good restaurant and a small school.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Želiš li ići na kavu sutra?
lines.en: Do you want to go for a coffee tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Hoću! Mogu sutra u deset sati.
lines.en: I do! I can tomorrow at ten o'clock.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super, vidimo se sutra! Bok!
lines.en: Great, see you tomorrow! Bye!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Vidimo se! Bok!
lines.en: See you! Bye!

22.title: u/na + locative: saying where you are
22.intro: The locative answers 'gdje?' (where, static location) and always comes with a preposition. The rule is small: masculine and neuter nouns add -u, feminine -a becomes -i. Use u for 'in/inside' and na for surfaces, open spaces and activities. Contrast with motion: Idem u grad (accusative) vs. U gradu sam (locative).

items.note: Masculine: add -u. U gradu sam. = I am in the city.
Place names decline like any noun: Živim u Zagrebu.
Irregular stem: posao → posl- (na poslu, not 'na posau'). Work takes na.
Feminine: -a → -i. Ana je u školi.
But 'at home' is the fixed phrase kod kuće.
Neuter: add -u. The sea takes na: Ljeti smo na moru.
Sound change: k → c before -i (banka → banci). Same in ruka → ruci, knjiga → knjizi.
Open spaces take na: na trgu, na ulici.
Čekam te na kolodvoru. = I'm waiting for you at the station.

items.hr: Idem u grad. / U gradu sam.
items.en: I'm going to town. / I'm in town.
items.note: Motion = u + accusative (grad); location = u + locative (gradu). Same preposition, different case.

22.title: kod and pored: two more place words
22.intro: Two everyday prepositions that take the genitive (a preview — the genitive gets its own day later). Learn these as ready-made chunks.

items.note: kod + genitive = at someone's place.
Fixed phrase — use it whenever you mean 'at home': Danas sam kod kuće.
pored + genitive = next to, beside.

items.en: I'm staying at a friend's place, next to the station.
items.note: stanovati → stanujem (-ovati verbs switch to -ujem).

22.title: Where is it? — locative drill
22.intro: Transform nouns into the locative and pick u or na. Watch for the two tricky ones: posao → poslu and banka → banci.

questions.prompt: Živim ___ Zagrebu.
questions.explanation: Cities take u: u Zagrebu, u Splitu, u gradu.

questions.prompt: Radim na ___. (posao — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: posao has the irregular stem posl-: na poslu = at work.

questions.prompt: Ljeti smo ___ moru.
questions.explanation: The sea takes na: na moru = at the seaside.

questions.prompt: Ana je u ___. (škola — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -i in the locative: škola → u školi.

questions.prompt: Novac je u ___. (banka)
questions.explanation: banka → banci: k changes to c before the locative -i (sibilarization).

questions.prompt: Sada sam u ___. (grad — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns add -u: grad → u gradu.

questions.prompt: Idem u ___. (grad — motion, going TO town)
questions.explanation: Motion uses u + accusative. Masculine inanimate accusative = nominative: Idem u grad. Location would be U gradu sam.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: My sister works at work. (Moja sestra…)
questions.explanation: Subject (Moja sestra) + verb (radi) + place (na poslu, locative).

questions.prompt: What does 'kod prijatelja' mean?
questions.explanation: kod + genitive = at someone's place: kod prijatelja, kod bake, kod kuće (at home).

questions.prompt: Ljeti smo na ___. (more — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: Neuter nouns add -u: more → na moru.

23.title: The seven days + today, tomorrow, yesterday
23.intro: Days of the week are NOT capitalized in Croatian. 'On Monday' = u + accusative: u ponedjeljak, u subotu. The week starts with Monday.

items.note: u ponedjeljak = on Monday (u + accusative).
u utorak = on Tuesday.
Feminine, so: u srijedu = on Wednesday.
From četiri (four) — the fourth day. u četvrtak.
From pet (five) — the fifth day. u petak.
u subotu = on Saturday.
u nedjelju = on Sunday. Careful: tjedan = week, nedjelja = Sunday.
Danas je srijeda, sutra je četvrtak.
Što radiš za vikend? = What are you doing at the weekend?

23.title: The twelve months
23.intro: Croatian month names are native Slavic words, not Latin ones — kolovoz is not 'August-ish', so they must be learned. 'In January' = u + locative: u siječnju. Months are not capitalized either.

items.note: u siječnju = in January (locative).
The only feminine month: u veljači.
u ožujku.
From trava (grass). u travnju.
u svibnju.
From lipa (linden tree). u lipnju.
From srp (sickle) — harvest time. u srpnju.
u kolovozu.
u rujnu.
Literally 'leaf-fall'. u listopadu.
Literally 'the cold one' — an adjective form: u studenom.
u prosincu.

23.title: Days & months drill
23.intro: Order of days, u + day for schedules, and the native month names.

questions.prompt: Which day comes after srijeda?
questions.explanation: ponedjeljak, utorak, srijeda, četvrtak (from četiri, four — the fourth day).

questions.prompt: Danas je petak, sutra je ___.
questions.explanation: After Friday (petak) comes Saturday (subota).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'on Monday'?
questions.explanation: 'On + day' is u + accusative: u ponedjeljak, u srijedu, u subotu.

questions.prompt: The first month of the year is ___.
questions.explanation: siječanj = January. In January = u siječnju.

questions.prompt: Which month comes after srpanj (July)?
questions.explanation: srpanj = July, kolovoz = August, rujan = September.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: On Saturday I'm going to the market.
questions.explanation: Time first (U subotu, u + accusative), then verb, then destination (na tržnicu, motion = accusative).

questions.prompt: Vikend (the weekend) consists of which two days?
questions.explanation: The weekend = subota (Saturday) and nedjelja (Sunday).

questions.prompt: 'In December' = u ___. (prosinac — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: prosinac has a fleeting a: prosinac → prosinc- + u = u prosincu.

questions.prompt: 'Rođendan mi je u svibnju.' — In which month is the birthday?
questions.explanation: svibanj = May; u svibnju = in May.

24.title: My day in order: the routine chain
24.intro: One story, nine sentences. The sequencers prvo (first), zatim (then/next), onda (then), na kraju (finally) glue your routine together — memorize the whole chain and you can narrate any day. All present tense.

items.en: First I get up at seven o'clock.
items.note: ustajati → ustajem. prvo = first.

items.hr: Zatim se tuširam i oblačim se.
items.en: Then I shower and get dressed.
items.note: Reflexive se sits in second position: Zatim se tuširam.

items.en: Then I have breakfast and drink coffee.
items.note: doručkovati → doručkujem (-ovati verbs switch to -ujem).

items.en: At eight I go to work.
items.note: Motion: na posao (accusative). Being there: na poslu (locative).

items.en: I work from nine to five.
items.note: od … do … = from … to …
ručati = to have lunch; u podne = at noon.

items.en: After work I go to the shop.
items.note: poslije + genitive: poslije posla = after work.

items.en: In the evening I have dinner and watch TV.
items.note: večerati = to have dinner. ujutro / popodne / navečer = in the morning / afternoon / evening.

items.hr: Na kraju idem spavati u jedanaest.
items.en: Finally I go to sleep at eleven.
items.note: na kraju = finally, in the end. ići spavati = to go to sleep.

24.title: Routine & sequencers drill
24.intro: Sequence words, routine verbs and their sometimes-surprising present forms (doručkujem!).

questions.prompt: Prvo ustajem, ___ doručkujem, na kraju idem spavati.
questions.explanation: The sequence chain: prvo → onda/zatim → na kraju.

questions.prompt: doručkovati — 'I have breakfast' = ___
questions.explanation: -ovati verbs switch to -ujem in the present: doručkovati → doručkujem, putovati → putujem.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'at noon'?
questions.explanation: u podne = at noon; ujutro = in the morning; navečer = in the evening; u ponoć = at midnight.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: First I get up at seven o'clock.
questions.explanation: Sequencer first (Prvo), then verb, then time (u sedam sati).

questions.prompt: Radim ___ devet do pet. (from nine to five)
questions.explanation: od … do … = from … to …: Radim od devet do pet.

questions.prompt: Ujutro idem ___. (I go TO work)
questions.explanation: Motion = na + accusative (na posao). Location = na + locative (na poslu). Work takes na, not u.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: In the evening I have dinner and watch TV.
questions.explanation: Time word first, two verbs joined with i, accusative object (televizija → televiziju).

questions.prompt: What does 'ručam' mean?
questions.explanation: ručati = to have lunch (ručak = lunch); doručkovati = breakfast, večerati = dinner.

questions.prompt: Zatim ___ tuširam. (Then I shower — add the missing little word)
questions.explanation: tuširati se is reflexive; the clitic se stands in second position: Zatim se tuširam.

25.title: Quantities & shopping phrases
25.intro: Quantity words (malo, puno, kilogram, litra, pola…) are followed by the genitive: kruh → kruha, voda → vode, mlijeko → mlijeka. Don't worry — the genitive gets its own day later; today learn these as ready chunks, plus the fixed market phrases.

items.note: Quantity + genitive: kruh → kruha (m. adds -a).
voda → vode (f. -a → -e).
Genitive plural: jabuke → jabuka.
pola + genitive: pola kilograma, pola litre.
mlijeko → mlijeka (n. adds -a).
deka (decagrams) is how Croatians order at the counter: deset deka = 100 g.
Molim vas + what you want — the universal polite request.
As a question, Izvolite? = 'What can I get you?' — the standard seller's opener.

items.en: How much does it cost? / How much do the apples cost?
items.note: Singular subject → košta; plural subject → koštaju.

items.hr: Želite li još nešto? — Ne, hvala, to je sve.
items.en: Would you like anything else? — No thanks, that's all.
items.note: Your ready-made exit from any shop conversation.
The genitive here means 'some/any' — a very Croatian touch.

25.title: Shopping & quantities drill
25.intro: Quantity + genitive chunks and the market phrases you'll need in the dialogue.

questions.prompt: malo ___ (kruh — a little bread)
questions.explanation: Quantity words take the genitive: masculine nouns add -a (kruh → kruha).

questions.prompt: puno ___ (voda — a lot of water)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -e in the genitive: voda → vode. (vodu is the accusative: Pijem vodu.)

questions.prompt: The seller says 'Izvolite?' — what do they mean?
questions.explanation: Izvolite? opens the exchange; Izvolite. (statement) = 'Here you are' when handing something over.

questions.prompt: Build the polite request: A kilo of apples, please.
questions.explanation: Molim vas + quantity + genitive plural (jabuke → jabuka).

questions.prompt: litra ___ (mlijeko — a litre of milk)
questions.explanation: Neuter nouns add -a in the genitive: mlijeko → mlijeka.

questions.prompt: 'Želite li još nešto?' — the polite way to say no:
questions.explanation: 'No thanks, that's all.' — the standard closing line at any counter.

questions.prompt: Why is it 'Koliko koštaju jabuke?' but 'Koliko košta sir?'
options: jabuke is plural, sir is singular
jabuke is feminine, sir is masculine
questions.answer: jabuke is plural, sir is singular
questions.explanation: The verb agrees with the subject: jabuke koštaju (they cost), sir košta (it costs).

questions.prompt: pola kilograma ___ (sir — half a kilo of cheese)
questions.explanation: Another quantity chunk: pola kilograma + genitive (sir → sira).

questions.prompt: 'Sve zajedno četiri eura i pedeset centi.' — how much do you pay?
questions.explanation: četiri = 4, pedeset = 50: four euros and fifty cents. sve zajedno = all together.

25.title: At the market (Na tržnici)
25.intro: A full shopping scene at a Zagreb market stall. Act out both roles, then re-run it swapping in different foods and quantities. Notice every quantity is followed by the genitive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Izvolite?
lines.en: Good day! What can I get you?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Molim vas kilogram jabuka.
lines.en: Good day! A kilo of apples, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izvolite. Želite li još nešto?
lines.en: Here you are. Would you like anything else?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Da, molim pola kilograma sira i dvadeset deka šunke.
lines.en: Yes, half a kilo of cheese and 200 grams of ham, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Imamo i svjež kruh.
lines.en: We also have fresh bread.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Super! Jedan kruh, molim. I malo rajčica.
lines.en: Great! One loaf, please. And a few tomatoes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Koliko rajčica želite?
lines.en: How many tomatoes would you like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Pola kilograma, molim. Koliko košta sve zajedno?
lines.en: Half a kilo, please. How much is everything together?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sve zajedno devet eura i dvadeset centi.
lines.en: All together nine euros and twenty cents.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Izvolite deset eura.
lines.en: Here's ten euros.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hvala! Izvolite ostatak. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you! Here's your change. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Hvala lijepa! Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much! Goodbye!

26.title: Eight connectors: from phrases to real sentences
26.intro: Connectors are the glue that turns two short sentences into one real thought. Croatian's core eight cover almost everything: i, a, ali, ili, jer, pa, onda, zato. Note the subtle one: a sits between i (and) and ali (but) — a mild contrast.

items.en: and — I drink coffee and tea.
items.note: Joins two alike things.

items.hr: a — Ja radim, a ti učiš.
items.en: and/whereas — I work, and (whereas) you study.
items.note: Mild contrast: stronger than i, weaker than ali. Very common.

items.en: but — I'm tired, but I'm working.
items.note: Direct opposition.

items.hr: ili — Želiš li kavu ili čaj?
items.en: or — Do you want coffee or tea?
items.note: Also ili … ili … = either … or …

items.hr: jer — Učim hrvatski jer živim u Zagrebu.
items.en: because — I'm learning Croatian because I live in Zagreb.
items.note: Never start an answer to 'Zašto?' without it: Zašto? — Jer…

items.hr: pa — Prvo radim, pa idem kući.
items.en: so/and then — First I work, and then I go home.
items.note: Loose, conversational 'so/then'.

items.hr: onda — Doručkujem, onda idem na posao.
items.en: then — I have breakfast, then I go to work.
items.note: You know it from sequencing; it also means 'in that case': Onda dobro!

items.hr: zato — Pada kiša, zato sam kod kuće.
items.en: that's why — It's raining, that's why I'm at home.
items.note: zato = that's why; zato što = because (synonym of jer).

items.hr: Ne govorim dobro, ali učim svaki dan jer želim živjeti u Hrvatskoj.
items.en: I don't speak well, but I study every day because I want to live in Croatia.
items.note: Three clauses, two connectors — this is what 'longer sentences' means.

26.title: Connector drill: join the ideas
26.intro: Pick the connector that fits the logic: addition, contrast, choice, reason or sequence.

questions.prompt: Umoran sam, ___ radim. (I'm tired, BUT I'm working)
questions.explanation: Direct opposition = ali (but).

questions.prompt: Učim hrvatski ___ živim u Zagrebu. (because)
questions.explanation: jer = because. zato što also works: Učim hrvatski zato što živim u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Želiš li kavu ___ čaj?
questions.explanation: A choice between two options = ili (or).

questions.prompt: Ja radim, ___ ti učiš. (mild contrast: I work, whereas you study)
questions.explanation: a marks mild contrast between two parallel statements — between i and ali in strength.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I'm learning Croatian because I live in Zagreb.
questions.answer: Učim hrvatski jer živim u Zagrebu
questions.explanation: Main clause + jer + reason clause. u Zagrebu = locative (location).

questions.prompt: Pada kiša, ___ sam kod kuće. (that's why)
questions.explanation: zato = that's why (result). jer would give the reason: Kod kuće sam jer pada kiša.

questions.prompt: Prvo radim, ___ idem kući. (so/and then — the loose conversational one)
questions.explanation: pa = so/and then. onda is also accepted — both sequence the two actions.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I don't speak well, but I'm learning.
questions.explanation: ne + verb for negation, ali for the contrast. (In writing there'd be a comma before ali.)

questions.prompt: Pijem kavu ___ čaj. (simple addition: coffee AND tea)
questions.explanation: Plain addition of two alike things = i.

27.title: Phrases that save a conversation
27.intro: Listening day. Before training your ear, arm yourself with the repair kit — the phrases that keep a conversation alive when you don't understand. These are among the most valuable sentences you will ever learn.

items.note: With rising intonation — the quickest way to ask for a repeat.
Formal (vi). To a friend: Možeš li ponoviti?
Your best friend in week one in Croatia.
razumjeti → razumijem. Say it early and often.
Signal you're following — keeps the other person talking.
značiti = to mean. Point at any word and ask.

items.en: How do you say "apple" in Croatian?
items.note: The reverse question — mines new vocabulary from any conversation.
Croatian is phonetic: once you see it written, you can say it.

27.title: Ear training: listen and answer
27.intro: Most questions here are audio-only: the sentence is spoken aloud and hidden. Listen, then answer. Croatian spelling is phonetic — if you can hear it, you can write it.

questions.prompt: Listen: where and when is this person going?
questions.explanation: Idem na more u subotu. = I'm going to the seaside on Saturday. (na more = motion; u subotu = on Saturday)

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear.
questions.explanation: hvala = thank you. The h is a light 'kh' sound.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (Careful with the first letter!)
questions.explanation: škola = school. š = the 'sh' sound — and it must be written with the haček.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (A common goodbye.)
questions.explanation: doviđenja = goodbye. The đ is the soft 'j' of 'jeans' — one letter, not 'dj'.

questions.prompt: Listen: where does Ana work?
questions.explanation: Ana radi u banci, a živi u Zagrebu. — She WORKS in a bank (u banci) and LIVES in Zagreb. Don't confuse radi and živi.
questions.audioText: Ana radi u banci, a živi u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: The word 'kuća' (house) contains which letter?
questions.explanation: kuća has the soft ć. Minimal pairs like č/ć are the hardest sounds to hear — listen for the softer, more palatal ć.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (It means 'woman'.)
questions.explanation: žena = woman. ž = the 's' in 'pleasure', written with a haček.

questions.prompt: Shadow the repair phrase: Can you repeat, please?
questions.explanation: Verb + li makes the question: Možete li ponoviti? Then the politeness tag molim vas.

questions.prompt: Listen: when is this person going to the cinema?
questions.explanation: Sutra idem u kino. = Tomorrow I'm going to the cinema.

28.title: The perfekt: biti + participle
28.intro: The everyday past tense of spoken Croatian = the short present of biti (sam, si, je…) + the past participle. The participle is easy: replace the infinitive -ti with -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n). A handful of key verbs are irregular — learn them as words.

items.note: The rule: cut -ti, add -o/-la/-lo. radi-ti → radi-o.
gleda-ti → gleda-o / gleda-la.
Irregular: the s drops (not 'jesao').
Irregular. Jučer sam bio u gradu. = Yesterday I was in town.
Irregular: ići → išao (m) / išla (f). The most useful irregular of all.
The participle shows YOUR gender — men say radio, women say radila.
sam is a clitic: it must stand in second position, right after the first word.

28.title: Perfekt word order, questions and negation
28.intro: The auxiliary (sam, si, je…) is a clitic — it can never stand first. Questions use je + li → 'Jesi li…?', and the negative forms (nisam, nisi…) are stressed, so they CAN stand first.

items.note: First word (Jučer), then the clitic sam, then the participle.
Participle first also works — sam still sits in second position.
Questions use the LONG form + li: Jesi li…? Jeste li…?

items.en: What did you do yesterday? (to a woman)
items.note: After a question word, the clitic si comes immediately: Što si…?
Negative biti + participle. Nisam is stressed, so it may stand first.

items.hr: Gdje si bio? — Bio sam u gradu.
items.en: Where were you? — I was in town.
items.note: Perfekt + locative: yesterday's grammar working together.

28.title: First steps in the past
28.intro: Form participles, place the clitic correctly, and build your first past-tense sentences.

questions.prompt: The past participle of raditi for a male speaker is:
questions.explanation: Cut -ti, add -o for masculine: raditi → radio. (radila = f, radilo = n)

questions.prompt: Jučer ___ učio hrvatski. (I studied — add the auxiliary)
questions.explanation: Perfekt = biti + participle. For ja the auxiliary is sam, in second position: Jučer sam učio.

questions.prompt: The participle of ići for a female speaker is:
questions.explanation: ići is irregular: išao (m), išla (f), išlo (n). Išla sam u grad. = I went to town (woman).

questions.prompt: Sinoć sam ___ film. (gledati — female speaker: Last night I watched a film)
questions.explanation: gledati → gledala for a female speaker: Sinoć sam gledala film.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Yesterday I worked at home. (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Jučer first, the clitic sam second, then the participle. kod kuće = at home.

questions.prompt: 'Gdje si bio jučer?' — which reply is correct?
questions.explanation: The clitic sam can't stand first — 'Sam bio…' is impossible. Bio sam u gradu puts it correctly in second position.

questions.prompt: Jutros sam ___ kavu. (piti — male speaker: This morning I drank coffee)
questions.explanation: piti → pio (m) / pila (f): Jutros sam pio kavu.

questions.prompt: The participle of jesti (to eat) for a male speaker is:
questions.explanation: jesti is irregular — the s drops: jeo (m), jela (f). Jeo sam sendvič.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Last night I watched television. (female speaker)
questions.explanation: Sinoć (last night) first, clitic sam second, female participle gledala, accusative object televiziju.

29.title: Participle agreement: -o, -la, -lo, -li, -le, -la
29.intro: The participle agrees with the SUBJECT in gender and number — a signature Slavic feature. Singular: -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n). Plural: -li (m or mixed group), -le (all-female), -la (neuter). Plus: negation with nisam, and the one quirk of reflexive verbs.

items.note: Your own gender decides: men -o, women -la.
Neuter subject → -lo.

items.en: We worked. (men or a mixed group)
items.note: Any group with at least one male → -li.
Only when everyone is female → -le.
Neuter plural → -la: sela su bila.

items.hr: Nisam radio. / Nisi radila. / Nije radilo.
items.en: I didn't work. / You didn't work. / It didn't work.
items.note: Negative = nisam/nisi/nije… + participle. Nisam can stand first.
Reflexive quirk: with se, the 3rd person singular drops je — never 'se je'.

items.hr: Marko i Ana su išli u kino.
items.en: Marko and Ana went to the cinema.
items.note: Mixed pair → -li.

items.hr: Ana i Iva su išle na more.
items.en: Ana and Iva went to the seaside.
items.note: Two women → -le.

29.title: Gender & number agreement drill
29.intro: Convert participles between genders and numbers, negate, and catch the reflexive trap.

questions.prompt: Ana i Marija su ___ u školu. (ići)
questions.explanation: An all-female group takes -le: Ana i Marija su išle.

questions.prompt: A woman says 'I worked yesterday': ___ sam jučer. (raditi)
questions.explanation: Female speaker → -la: Radila sam jučer.

questions.prompt: Marko i Ana su ___ cijeli dan. (raditi — a mixed pair)
questions.explanation: Any group containing a male takes -li, even one man among many women.

questions.prompt: How does a man say 'I didn't work'?
questions.explanation: ne + sam fuse into nisam; male speaker keeps -o: Nisam radio.

questions.prompt: Mi (grupa muškaraca — a group of men) smo ___ hrvatski. (učiti)
questions.explanation: Masculine plural → -li: Učili smo hrvatski.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct? (He laughed.)
questions.explanation: With reflexive se, the 3rd person singular auxiliary je is dropped: On se smijao — never 'se je'.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Ana and Iva went to school.
questions.answer: Ana i Iva su išle u školu
questions.explanation: All-female subject → išle. u školu = motion (accusative), not u školi.

questions.prompt: Dijete je ___. (spavati — The child slept)
questions.explanation: Neuter singular subject → -lo: Dijete je spavalo.

questions.prompt: 'Gdje ste bili?' asked to a group of women — the correct answer:
questions.explanation: All-female 'we' → bile: Bile smo na moru. (Men or a mixed group would say bili.)

30.title: Your milestone script: the building blocks
30.intro: Today you prove Phase 1: a real conversation covering (1) who you are, (2) your routine, (3) yesterday — in the perfekt. These nine frames are the skeleton; fill them with your own life, say each aloud, then run the dialogue below.

items.note: Your opener since day 3 — now it starts a real conversation.

items.hr: Imam trideset godina i živim u Zagrebu.
items.en: I am thirty years old and I live in Zagreb.
items.note: Age with imati + locative for where you live.

items.en: I've been learning Croatian for thirty days now.
items.note: već + time = 'for … already'. Croatian uses the present here.

items.hr: Svaki dan prvo ustajem, onda doručkujem i idem na posao.
items.en: Every day I first get up, then have breakfast and go to work.
items.note: Your day-24 routine chain, compressed to one sentence.

items.en: I work from nine to five.

items.hr: Jučer sam ustao u osam. / Jučer sam ustala u osam.
items.en: Yesterday I got up at eight. (m / f)
items.note: Routine verbs flip into the perfekt: ustati → ustao/ustala.

items.hr: Išao sam u grad i kupio sam kruh. / Išla sam u grad i kupila sam kruh.
items.en: I went to town and bought bread. (m / f)
items.note: ići → išao/išla; kupiti → kupio/kupila. Keep the participles agreeing with YOU.

items.hr: Navečer sam bio umoran. / Navečer sam bila umorna.
items.en: In the evening I was tired. (m / f)
items.note: Adjective agrees too: umoran (m) / umorna (f).

items.hr: Bilo je teško, ali sada mogu razgovarati na hrvatskom.
items.en: It was hard, but now I can have a conversation in Croatian.
items.note: Your closing line — and it's true.

30.title: Phase 1 milestone check
30.intro: Ten questions across everything from days 21-29: locative, days and months, routine, quantities, connectors and the perfekt. This is your A1 gate.

questions.prompt: 'I am in the city' (location, not motion):
questions.explanation: Static location = u + locative: u gradu. (u grad = accusative, motion: Idem u grad.)

questions.prompt: Jučer ___ kupila kruh. (a woman: Yesterday I bought bread)
questions.explanation: Perfekt auxiliary for ja = sam, in second position: Jučer sam kupila kruh.

questions.prompt: Which day comes after petak?
questions.explanation: petak (Friday) → subota (Saturday).

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Yesterday I went to the market. (female speaker)
questions.explanation: Jučer + clitic sam (second position) + išla (f) + na tržnicu (motion, accusative).

questions.prompt: malo ___ (kruh — a little bread)
questions.explanation: Quantity + genitive: kruh → kruha.

questions.prompt: Prvo doručkujem, ___ idem na posao, na kraju spavam.
questions.explanation: The sequence chain: prvo → onda → na kraju.

questions.prompt: 'Što ste radili jučer?' — the best answer (male speaker):
options: Učio sam hrvatski i gledao sam film.
Učila sam hrvatski i gledala sam film.
questions.answer: Učio sam hrvatski i gledao sam film.
questions.explanation: Past question → perfekt answer, male participles (-o), and the clitic sam never first. The present-tense option answers a different question.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ u Zagrebu. (biti — female speaker: Yesterday I was in Zagreb)
questions.explanation: biti → bio (m) / bila (f): Jučer sam bila u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I work from nine to five.
questions.explanation: od … do … = from … to …: Radim od devet do pet.

questions.prompt: 'u kolovozu' means:
questions.explanation: kolovoz = August (a native Croatian month name); u kolovozu = in August (locative).

30.title: The milestone conversation: who I am, my day, yesterday
30.intro: The Phase 1 milestone, as a full script: introduce yourself, describe your routine, and tell what you did yesterday — in the perfekt. The script uses female participle forms (ustala, kupila…); male speakers swap -la for -o (ustao, kupio). Run it once as written, then again with your own details, ideally with a tutor or partner.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Drago mi je. Recite mi nešto o sebi — tko ste vi?
lines.en: Good day! Nice to meet you. Tell me something about yourself — who are you?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Zovem se Ana. Imam trideset godina i živim u Zagrebu. Učim hrvatski već trideset dana.
lines.en: My name is Ana. I'm thirty years old and I live in Zagreb. I've been learning Croatian for thirty days now.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično! Kakav je vaš dan? Što radite svaki dan?
lines.en: Excellent! What is your day like? What do you do every day?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Prvo ustajem u sedam, onda doručkujem i pijem kavu. Zatim idem na posao.
lines.en: First I get up at seven, then I have breakfast and drink coffee. Then I go to work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A poslije posla?
lines.en: And after work?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Poslije posla idem u trgovinu. Navečer večeram, učim hrvatski i gledam televiziju.
lines.en: After work I go to the shop. In the evening I have dinner, study Croatian and watch TV.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. A što ste radili jučer?
lines.en: Great. And what did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Jučer sam ustala u osam. Doručkovala sam i išla sam u grad.
lines.en: Yesterday I got up at eight. I had breakfast and went to town.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ste kupili?
lines.en: What did you buy?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Kupila sam kruh, sir i kilogram jabuka na tržnici.
lines.en: I bought bread, cheese and a kilo of apples at the market.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jeste li učili hrvatski jučer?
lines.en: Did you study Croatian yesterday?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Jesam! Učila sam jedan sat i slušala sam radio. Navečer sam bila umorna i spavala sam.
lines.en: I did! I studied for an hour and listened to the radio. In the evening I was tired and I slept.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Sada govorite hrvatski. Čestitam!
lines.en: Bravo! You speak Croatian now. Congratulations!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Hvala vam! Bilo je teško, ali sada mogu razgovarati na hrvatskom.
lines.en: Thank you! It was hard, but now I can have a conversation in Croatian.
